source_paper_title: Stochastic Games with General Payoff Functions
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Flesch_2024, title={Stochastic Games with General Payoff Functions}, volume={49}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.1385}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.1385}, number={3}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Flesch, János and Solan, Eilon}, year={2024}, month=aug, pages={1349–1371} }
context:
Page 2, Introduction (end of item [4] discussion)

Our result connects with and gives a very partial answer to the long-standing problem of whether every multiplayer stochastic game with finite action spaces, finite or countably infinite state space, and bounded and Borel-measurable payoffs admits an \(\varepsilon\)-equilibrium for every \(\varepsilon>0\).
title: Existence of epsilon-equilibria in multiplayer stochastic games with Borel payoffs
background_latex:
A (multiplayer) stochastic game is a tuple \(\Gamma=(I,S,(A_i)_{i\in I},p,(f_i)_{i\in I})\) where:
1) \(I\) is a finite nonempty set of players.
2) \(S\) is a finite or countably infinite set of states.
3) For each \(i\in I\), \(A_i\) is a finite nonempty action set; write \(A:=\prod_{i\in I} A_i\).
4) \(p:S\times A\to \Delta(S)\) is a transition kernel; when the current state is \(s\in S\) and action profile \(a\in A\) is played, the next state \(s'\in S\) is drawn according to \(p(\cdot\mid s,a)\).
5) A run (infinite play) is an infinite sequence \(r=(s_1,a_1,s_2,a_2,\dots)\in (S\times A)^\infty\) consistent with \(p\); let \(R\) denote the set of all runs. Each \(f_i:R\to\mathbb{R}\) is bounded and Borel-measurable (with respect to the product topology on \((S\times A)^\infty\) and the induced Borel \(\sigma\)-algebra on \(R\)).

A (behavior) strategy for player \(i\) is a mapping \(\sigma_i:H\to\Delta(A_i)\), where \(H\) is the set of all finite histories \((s_1,a_1,\dots,s_n)\) consistent with \(p\). A strategy profile is \(\sigma=(\sigma_i)_{i\in I}\). For each initial state \(s\in S\) and strategy profile \(\sigma\), the induced probability measure on runs is denoted \(\mathbb{P}_{s,\sigma}\) and expectation \(\mathbb{E}_{s,\sigma}\).

For \(\varepsilon\ge 0\), a strategy profile \(\sigma^*\) is an \(\varepsilon\)-equilibrium (Nash equilibrium with additive \(\varepsilon\)-slack) if for every initial state \(s\in S\), every player \(i\in I\), and every alternative strategy \(\sigma_i\),
\[\mathbb{E}_{s,\sigma^*}[f_i]\ge \mathbb{E}_{s,(\sigma_i,\sigma^*_{-i})}[f_i]-\varepsilon.\]
Equivalently, no player can improve expected payoff by more than \(\varepsilon\) via a unilateral deviation, from any specified initial state.
problem_definition_latex:
Determine whether the following statement holds:

For every stochastic game \(\Gamma=(I,S,(A_i)_{i\in I},p,(f_i)_{i\in I})\) with finite action sets \((A_i)_{i\in I}\), finite or countably infinite state space \(S\), and bounded Borel-measurable payoff functions \((f_i)_{i\in I}\), and for every \(\varepsilon>0\), there exists a strategy profile \(\sigma^*\) that is an \(\varepsilon\)-equilibrium for every initial state \(s\in S\); that is,
\[\forall s\in S,\ \forall i\in I,\ \forall \sigma_i,\quad \mathbb{E}_{s,\sigma^*}[f_i]\ge \mathbb{E}_{s,(\sigma_i,\sigma^*_{-i})}[f_i]-\varepsilon.\]

A (multiplayer) stochastic game is a tuple \(\Gamma=(I,S,(A_i)_{i\in I},p,(f_i)_{i\in I})\) where:
1) \(I\) is a finite nonempty set of players.
2) \(S\) is a finite or countably infinite set of states.
3) For each \(i\in I\), \(A_i\) is a finite nonempty action set; write \(A:=\prod_{i\in I} A_i\).

Each \(f_i:R\to\mathbb{R}\) is bounded and Borel-measurable (with respect to the product topology on \((S\times A)^\infty\) and the induced Borel \(\sigma\)-algebra on \(R\)).

A strategy profile is \(\sigma=(\sigma_i)_{i\in I}\). For each initial state \(s\in S\) and strategy profile \(\sigma\), the induced probability measure on runs is denoted \(\mathbb{P}_{s,\sigma}\) and expectation \(\mathbb{E}_{s,\sigma}\).

For \(\varepsilon\ge 0\), a strategy profile \(\sigma^*\) is an \(\varepsilon\)-equilibrium (Nash equilibrium with additive \(\varepsilon\)-slack) if for every initial state \(s\in S\), every player \(i\in I\), and every alternative strategy \(\sigma_i\),

Determine whether the following statement holds:

For every stochastic game \(\Gamma=(I,S,(A_i)_{i\in I},p,(f_i)_{i\in I})\) with finite action sets \((A_i)_{i\in I}\), finite or countably infinite state space \(S\), and bounded Borel-measurable payoff functions \((f_i)_{i\in I}\), and for every \(\varepsilon>0\), there exists a strategy profile \(\sigma^*\) that is an \(\varepsilon\)-equilibrium for every initial state \(s\in S\); that is,

open_problem_quote: Our result connects with and gives a very partial answer to the long-standing problem of whether every multiplayer stochastic game with finite action spaces, finite or countably infinite state space, and bounded and Borel-measurable payoffs admits an \(\varepsilon\)-equilibrium for every \(\varepsilon>0\).
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The problem is a universal existence question over all bounded Borel-measurable payoff functions on infinite plays, so there is no clear finite parametrized instance family to exhaustively search. While one can search for counterexamples within restricted subclasses, failure to find one provides little evidence for the general statement.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: An evolutionary search could explore structured families of stochastic games and payoff specifications to hunt for counterexamples or to test candidate equilibrium-construction templates via approximate best-response computations. However, the full Borel-measurable generality and need for rigorous existence proofs limit the faithfulness of any computable fitness proxy.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The question asks for existence, for every >0, of a (behavior) strategy profile ^* that is an -equilibrium simultaneously for every initial state in a multiplayer stochastic game with finite actions, countable states, and bounded Borel payoff functions on the run space . The 2024 paper by Flesch and Solan places this as a long-standing open problem and develops a unifying approach based on a multiplayer extension of Martin's auxiliary-game method: for each player  and >0 they construct a bounded Martin function ^_:\to\mathbb{R} satisfying _i(h)-\varepsilon\le D_i^\varepsilon(h)\le v_i(h) and a local-to-global property: playing near-optimally in the induced one-shot games (D_i^\varepsilon,h) yields global guarantees in every subgame.

Using this machinery, the strongest general positive results currently available fall short of a global Nash -equilibrium for all initial states. Flesch and Solan prove (i) existence of subgame -maxmin strategies for each player, (ii) existence of minmax -acceptable profiles ensuring \mathbb{E}[f_i]\ge v_i(h)-\varepsilon in every subgame, (iii) existence of extensive-form correlated -equilibria, and (iv) existence of an -solvable subgame (some history  such that the subgame _h admits an -equilibrium). Item (iv) uses, in an essential way, deviation-detection techniques (cited as Alon et al.) to enforce incentives against deviations from nonstationary prescriptions.

Earlier foundational work of Martin and of Maitra--Sudderth establishes determinacy/value existence for two-player zero-sum Borel-payoff stochastic games, which is the conceptual origin of the Martin-function decomposition into one-shot games. Subsequent partial progress toward multiplayer equilibrium existence proceeds by imposing structure: repeated games with tail-measurable payoffs (single state) admit -equilibria via a generalized Martin function, and special stochastic-game classes such as two-player absorbing games with tail payoffs or two-player finite-state games with shift-invariant payoffs also admit -equilibria. The remaining gap is precisely the full multiplayer, countable-state, Borel-payoff setting without additional regularity: current techniques can ensure individual rationality in all subgames and can find at least one subgame where an -equilibrium exists, but do not yet assemble these ingredients into a single strategy profile that is an -equilibrium for every initial state.

sources.title: The determinacy of Blackwell games
sources.theorem_or_result: Proves determinacy for Blackwell games with Borel payoff sets, yielding existence of a value and optimal (or ���-approximate) strategies in two-player zero-sum stochastic games with Borel payoffs via reduction to auxiliary one-shot games (the Martin function approach).
sources.connection: Provides the foundational determinacy/auxiliary-game technique that underlies essentially all known positive results for Borel-payoff stochastic games; however it is limited to two-player zero-sum settings and does not yield Nash �-equilibria in general multiplayer games.

sources.title: Finitely additive stochastic games with Borel measurable payoffs
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops a finitely additive probability framework for stochastic games with Borel payoffs and establishes value/determinacy-type results for two-player zero-sum games, complementing Martin's approach.
sources.connection: Another cornerstone for handling Borel payoffs in stochastic games; informs later extensions (including Flesch--Solan) but does not resolve multiplayer �-equilibrium existence.

sources.title: Equilibrium in multiplayer repeated games with tail-measurable payoffs
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces a multiplayer generalization of the Martin function and uses it to prove existence of ���-equilibria in multiplayer repeated games (single state) with tail-measurable payoffs.
sources.connection: Shows the Martin-function program can yield ���-equilibria beyond two-player zero-sum, but only in the repeated-game (no state dynamics) and tail-measurable payoff setting; it is a key stepping stone toward the full stochastic-game problem.

sources.title: Equilibrium in two-player absorbing games with tail-measurable payoffs
sources.theorem_or_result: Applies the generalized Martin-function method to establish ���-equilibrium existence in two-player absorbing games with tail-measurable payoffs.
sources.connection: Provides a positive result in a nontrivial stochastic-game subclass (absorbing structure) with general (tail) payoffs, indicating where equilibrium existence can be proved and what structural properties may be exploited.

sources.title: Equilibrium in two-player stochastic games with shift-invariant payoffs
sources.theorem_or_result: Proves existence of ���-equilibria in two-player stochastic games with finite state space under shift-invariant payoff functions, using Martin-function ideas.
sources.connection: Establishes equilibrium existence for a broad class of non-discounted, non-average payoffs in a two-player finite-state setting; highlights that even for two players, additional payoff regularity (shift-invariance) can substitute for full Borel generality.

sources.title: Detecting deviations in nonstationary strategies (Alon et al.)
sources.theorem_or_result: Provides a probabilistic/algorithmic tool to detect deviations from prescribed nonstationary play with high confidence, enabling enforcement arguments in long-horizon dynamic games.
sources.connection: Used essentially in Flesch--Solan (2024) to prove existence of an ���-solvable subgame; such deviation-detection tools may be necessary for any attempt to build global ���-equilibria under Borel payoffs.

sources.title: Stochastic games
sources.theorem_or_result: Establishes existence of the value for two-player zero-sum stochastic games with finite state and action spaces under long-run average payoff, introducing influential dynamic programming and uniform value techniques.
sources.connection: Represents the classical equilibrium/value theory for special payoff functions; contrasts with the Borel-payoff setting where such methods fail and motivates the need for Martin-function/determinacy methods.

sources.title: Stochastic Games with General Payoff Functions
sources.theorem_or_result: For multiplayer stochastic games with finite actions, countable states, and bounded Borel payoffs, proves: (i) subgame ���-maxmin strategies for each player; (ii) minmax ���-acceptable profiles; (iii) existence of extensive-form correlated ���-equilibria; (iv) existence of an ���-solvable subgame (some history whose subgame admits an ���-equilibrium).
sources.connection: Directly addresses the open problem by providing the strongest currently known general existence statements short of a global ���-equilibrium for every initial state; explicitly frames the full ���-equilibrium existence as open and gives a partial answer via ���-solvable subgames and correlated equilibria.
